title: Committed review suppresses unsupported diagnostic-status failure concerns

provenance.originSummary: Synthetic benchmark scenario designed to exercise the rule directly.
provenance.whyItMattered: Use this case to benchmark suppression of unsupported diagnostic-status failure concerns unless later implementation-backed evidence changes that judgment.
provenance.genericCondition: Synthetic fixture models a provider poller where `primary_status` is the authoritative readiness signal and `secondary_status` is diagnostic-only context. The benchmark is that review should suppress an unsupported failure-handling concern that relies only on the diagnostic field.

description: A synthetic committed-review scenario where an earlier run treated a diagnostic-only status field as an independent terminal-failure signal even though the implementation explicitly marks a different status field as authoritative. The correct benchmark is that review should not raise a failure-handling issue without new implementation-backed evidence.

scope.notes: Synthetic fixture models a provider poller where `primary_status` is the authoritative readiness signal and `secondary_status` is diagnostic-only context. The benchmark is that review should suppress an unsupported failure-handling concern that relies only on the diagnostic field.

mustCatch.description: The committed review should distinguish implementation-backed poller behavior from unsupported semantic speculation and should respect the in-code rationale that `primary_status` is authoritative while `secondary_status` is diagnostic-only context.

mustNotFlag.description: The review must not raise an Issue claiming the update poller can miss a terminal failure solely because it does not independently fail on `secondary_status == Failed` when the current implementation-backed evidence says `primary_status` is authoritative and `secondary_status` is diagnostic-only context.

notes: Use this case to benchmark suppression of unsupported diagnostic-status failure concerns unless later implementation-backed evidence changes that judgment.
